text: Example Rakia begins an eligible temporary relocation on December 28, 2024. She incurs eligible temporary relocation expenses in December 2024 of $1,000 for a round trip flight between her ordinary residence and the temporary lodging, $100 for meals during her travel time and $400 for temporary lodging near the temporary work location for a total amount of $1,500. Rakia does not receive any employment income related to the temporary work location until January, 2025. Therefore, Rakia would not be able to deduct her eligible temporary relocation expenses in 2024 since she does not have employment income in 2024 related to the temporary relocation. However, she could include the 2024 expenses when calculating her temporary relocation deduction for 2025.
title: T4044 – Employment Expenses

text: However, she could include the 2024 expenses when calculating her temporary relocation deduction for 2025. For 2024, Rakia would fill out “Line 11 – Calculation of labour mobility deduction” on her T777 as follows: ● Rakia must include all of her eligible temporary relocation expenses incurred prior to February 1, 2025 in calculating her eligible temporary relocation expenses for 2024 and carry forward the portion that she cannot claim as a LMD in 2024 (in this case, $1,500) ● Rakia can claim her flight and other transportation expenses for one-round trip from her ordinary residence to the temporary lodging, however, other transportation expenses while she is at the temporary work location are not eligible relocation expenses (for example, car rental while at the temporary location) ● If Rakia does not maintain her ordinary residence (for example, she sublets it to another person), only her transportation and meals for the round trip will qualify as eligible temporary relocation expenses See, on the next page, how Rakia would complete the section “Line 11 – Calculation of labour mobility deduction” on her T777 form for 2025.
title: T4044 – Employment Expenses

text: For 2024, Rakia would fill out “Line 11 – Calculation of labour mobility deduction” on her T777 as follows: ● Rakia must include all of her eligible temporary relocation expenses incurred prior to February 1, 2025 in calculating her eligible temporary relocation expenses for 2024 and carry forward the portion that she cannot claim as a LMD in 2024 (in this case, $1,500) ● Rakia can claim her flight and other transportation expenses for one-round trip from her ordinary residence to the temporary lodging, however, other transportation expenses while she is at the temporary work location are not eligible relocation expenses (for example, car rental while at the temporary location) ● If Rakia does not maintain her ordinary residence (for example, she sublets it to another person), only her transportation and meals for the round trip will qualify as eligible temporary relocation expenses See, on the next page, how Rakia would complete the section “Line 11 – Calculation of labour mobility deduction” on her T777 form for 2025. In 2025, Rakia earns $7,000 of employment income at the temporary work relocation.
title: T4044 – Employment Expenses

text: In 2025, Rakia earns $7,000 of employment income at the temporary work relocation. For this temporary relocation, she has no additional transportation expenses (as her round trip was paid for in 2024), $100 for meals consumed during the return flight between her temporary lodging and ordinary residence, and $650 in temporary lodging expenses totaling $750, plus the carry forward from 2024 of $1,500. Her total eligible temporary relocation expenses would be $2,250. She claimed her round trip flight in the carry forward at line 33. 20 canada.ca/taxes.

text: Example Wade is a salaried employee who worked from home from January 1 until December 31, 2024. Wade used a designated room that was 20% of the total square footage of his rented two bedroom apartment. Wade paid the following expenses for this period: ● Supplies: $75 ● Cell phone, long distance calls: $180 ● Electricity: $1,500 ● Heat: $800 ● Water: $1,000 ● Internet: $400 ● Maintenance: $60 (he painted an unfinished room and used it as his designated work space) ● Rent: $7,500 During that period Wade earned $4,000 from his employment. Wade calculates his employment-use amount and completes his Form T777 as follows: He totals his electricity, heat, water, internet ($3,700) and adds his rent ($7,500): $3,700 + $7,500 = $11,200.
title: T4044 – Employment Expenses

text: Wade calculates his employment-use amount and completes his Form T777 as follows: He totals his electricity, heat, water, internet ($3,700) and adds his rent ($7,500): $3,700 + $7,500 = $11,200. He then multiplies this amount by 20%: $11,200 x 20% = $2,240. Wade can add his maintenance costs of $60 to this amount because he is using a designated space: $2,240 + $60 = $2,300 (employment-use amount). Because of space limitations, the entire form is not reproduced. 24 canada.ca/taxes.

title: T4044 – Employment Expenses

text: Example Sam is a commissioned salesperson who negotiates contracts for his employer in Ontario which has an HST rate of 13%. Under his contract of employment, he has to pay his own expenses and is normally required to work away from his employer’s place of business. His employer is a GST/HST registrant. Sam received a taxable allowance for the use of his motor vehicle (purchased in December of 2023) that is included on his T4 slip for 2024. Since the allowance is taxable, he can claim a rebate on certain expenses related to that allowance. His travel for work is restricted to Ontario and all of his expenses are incurred within the province. To calculate his employment expenses, he completed Form T777, Statement of Employment Expenses, as shown below and on the top of the following page. Because of space limitations, the entire form is not reproduced. 38 canada.ca/taxes

To claim the rebate, he has to complete Form GST370, Employee and Partner GST/HST Rebate Application. Sam completes Part A. Before he can complete Part B, he has to complete Chart 1 of Form GST370 to calculate his HST eligible expenses. He also must complete Chart 2 because he is claiming CCA on his motor vehicle. Using the information in this guide, he calculates and reports the expenses not eligible for the rebate in column 2B of Chart 1. To calculate the personal-use portion of his motor vehicle expenses, Sam uses the fraction 7,500/30,000.
title: T4044 – Employment Expenses

text: To calculate the personal-use portion of his motor vehicle expenses, Sam uses the fraction 7,500/30,000. This is the personal kilometres driven (30,000 – 22,500) over the total kilometres driven. He enters this non-eligible portion of CCA in column 2B of Chart 2. He completes Chart 1 and Chart 2 on his Form GST370 as follows: * $3,230.55 × (7,500/30,000) = $807.64 *** $84.50 × (7,500/30,000) = $21.13 ** $467.67 × (7,500/30,000) = $116.92 **** $8,644.50 × (7,500/30,000) = $2,161.13 Sam did not enter any amounts in the black areas, since these expenses are not eligible for the rebate. canada.ca/taxes 39.

Sam enters $1,254.17 on line 45700 of his 2024 income tax and benefit return. He also attaches Form GST370 to his tax return. On his 2025 income tax and benefit return, Sam will include $508.29 ($4,418.25 × [13/113]) on line 10400. This amount is the part of the rebate he will receive in 2025 that relates to eligible expenses other than CCA.

text: If you received scholarships, fellowships, or bursaries in the tax year in connection with your part-time enrolment in an educational program, this chart will help you calculate the amount of scholarship, fellowship and bursary income to be included on line 13010 of your Income Tax and Benefit Return. Chart to calculate the portion of the award that must be included in income Calculation of scholarship exemption for the tax year: Scholarships, fellowships, and bursaries received by you in the tax year related to a part-time program for which you are a part-time qualifying student for 2023, 2024, or 2025. 1 Fees paid to the educational institution and costs of program-related material for the part-time program for which you are a part-time qualifying student for 2023, 2024, or 2025. Do not include costs and fees paid that you claimed for a scholarship exemption in 2 a previous year. Enter the amount from line 1 or line 2, whichever is less.

text: Enter the amount from line 1 or line 2, whichever is less. 3 Line 1 minus line 2 (if negative, enter “0”) 4 Basic scholarship exemption $500.00 5 Enter the amount from line 4 or line 5, whichever is less. 6 Add lines 3 and 6. text: Filing an objection – the communication of a revised PA amount via a NORA does not constitute a reassessment Why do you file? – a PA amount cannot be objected to as it is not an amount assessed or determined under the ITA If you think we have misinterpreted the facts or applied the law incorrectly, you have the right to object to: ■ late filed objections (filed beyond the time limit for filing an objection) ■ income tax assessments, reassessments and determinations indicating taxes or amounts owed ■ early filed objections (filed prior to the issuance of the Notice of assessment or determination) ■ determinations or redeterminations such as the goods and services tax / harmonized sales tax (GST/HST) credit, the These lists are not exhaustive.

text: Appendix A – Sample objection letter By fax: (see Appendix B) Your address and telephone number Date Chief of Appeals Appeals Intake Centre 1050 Notre Dame Avenue Sudbury ON P3A 5C1 Re: Objection to 2020 reassessment (give your account number) Dear Sir or Madam: I object to the reassessment of my 2020 income tax return. Please find enclosed a copy of my June 15, 2021, notice of reassessment. In July 2020, I moved from Vancouver to Edmundston, N.B. to start a new job. I claimed the moving expenses on my 2020 return and attached Form T1-M, Moving Expenses Deduction. As my T1-M form indicates, I drove to Edmundston with my family.
title: P148 – Newcomers – Getting Benefits and Credits

text: As my T1-M form indicates, I drove to Edmundston with my family. In my claim for transportation costs, I included the following costs of a side trip to Banff: travel costs (gas + car repair) $478.75 lodging (1 night) $175.00 meals (2 days) $205.50 The Canada Revenue Agency disallowed this part of my claim. Originally, I intended to take a day trip from Calgary to Banff. However, my car overheated on the way, and I had to stay overnight for radiator repairs. Since I could not control this situation, I think you should allow this cost as a moving expense. At the very least, you should let me claim the $443.75 car repair. I would be pleased to discuss this matter with you. Yours truly, Your name Enclosure 10 canada.ca/taxes.

text: Tax treaties Canada has tax conventions, agreements, and arrangements (commonly referred to as tax treaties) with many countries and regions. These tax treaties are designed to avoid double taxation for those who would otherwise have to pay tax in two countries or regions on the same income. Generally, tax treaties determine how much each country or region can tax income such as wages, salaries, pensions, and interest. For more information, go to canada.ca/cra-tax-treaties. If you receive Canadian-source employment income or Canadian self-employment business income exempt from tax in Canada because of a tax treaty, you can ask your employer or the payer not to withhold tax. Before your employer or the payer can stop withholding tax from your income, you need a waiver letter from the CRA. Send your request for a waiver letter to the International Waivers Centre of Expertise that serves your Canadian employer or the payer.
title: T4058 – Non-Residents and Income Tax

text: Send your request for a waiver letter to the International Waivers Centre of Expertise that serves your Canadian employer or the payer. If the officials at the tax office agree you qualify, they will send you a waiver letter to give to your employer or payer.

title: RC4210 – GST/HST Credit guide

text: Find out if this guide is for you This guide gives information about the goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax (GST/HST) credit, such as: ■ who is eligible ■ when you get it ■ how it is calculated ■ what are the related provincial and territorial programs administered by the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) This guide uses plain language to explain most common tax situations. It is provided for information only and does not replace the law. The CRA’s publications and personalized correspondence are available in braille, large print, e-text, and MP3.
